ARGUMENTS: In this repo we have a good workflow built out for intake, the checklist building, and then the LLC form fill out. This is our plan for the hackathon project

Project: Cross-language business onboarding agent for immigrant founders
The asymmetry. When a US-born founder starts a business, they get a consultation - someone who knows the system tells them what to file, in what order, and why. That's a $200-400 conversation with a lawyer, or an offhand chat with a family friend in business. The conversation surfaces obligations the founder didn't know to ask about: BOI reports, Cook County DBA rules, food service certification, sales tax registration, workers' comp pre-registration, the 60-day window for the EIN. First-generation immigrant founders systematically miss this consultation - they ask family who know partial answers, hit obligations reactively (often through fines), or operate informally for years to avoid the friction. The asymmetry isn't translation, it's the consultation that surfaces what your specific business actually needs. Translation tools and ChatGPT can answer questions you ask; they can't tell you what you didn't know to ask.
What we're building. A three-phase agent that delivers the consultation an immigrant founder doesn't have access to:
Phase 1 - Intake. User talks to the agent in Chinese about their business: what they do, where they're located, how they operate, hiring plans, products. Agent asks targeted follow-ups, builds a structured profile.
Phase 2 - Personalized obligation map. Agent generates a checklist of bureaucratic actions specific to that business, prioritized and sequenced, with citations to the underlying regulations (federal IRS, Illinois DOR, Cook County, City of Chicago). For a Chicago single-member restaurant LLC, this surfaces ~12 obligations - several of which the user almost certainly didn't know about. This is the primary wow moment of the demo: the asymmetry being closed in real time, visibly.
Phase 3 - Autonomous execution. Agent walks through checklist items by opening the relevant websites and filling forms autonomously while the user converses in Chinese. Fills the obvious 70% of fields silently; pauses at judgment fields, highlights them with bilingual annotations, asks the user a clarification question in Chinese with the relevant context. User responds by voice, agent fills the field, continues. At completion of each item, agent summarizes what was filed and surfaces the next checklist item.
Why this isn't ChatGPT, Google Translate, or Accent.
ChatGPT translates and advises but doesn't act, doesn't surface obligations you don't ask about, doesn't maintain state across a workflow, and isn't grounded in cited regulations.
Google Translate translates surface text but does none of the consultation, none of the operation, and none of the judgment-mediated decision making.
Accent guides ("here's the button to click") but doesn't fill forms, doesn't track multi-screen workflows, and doesn't operate across languages with cultural/legal context.
The interaction model is the deeper differentiator. Chat is structurally wrong for operating a foreign-language website while explaining things - it forces the user to alternate between two surfaces (chatbot tab + form tab) and translate between "what the chatbot said" and "where to do it on the form." Our overlay collapses these surfaces - the instruction is the location, rendered in place. The user speaks (low-effort, fast), the system acts (form fills, navigation). Voice in, action out is fundamentally different from text in, text out, especially when the user's working memory is already taxed by operating a foreign UI.
Demo flow. Pre-staged Chicago single-member restaurant LLC, ~3 minutes total.
0:00-0:30 - Phase 1 intake. Chinese-speaking restaurant owner describes her business in Chinese: name, location, plans to hire two staff, sells food but no alcohol. Agent asks two targeted follow-ups in Chinese, builds the profile.
0:30-1:00 - Phase 2 checklist reveal. Agent generates a 12-item checklist on screen, with Chinese explanations of each item and English citations. Items include: Illinois LLC formation, EIN application, BOI report, Illinois sales tax permit, Cook County DBA (if applicable), Chicago business license, restaurant license, food service sanitation certification, withholding registration (since hiring), workers' comp pre-registration, federal employment tax setup, IL-941 setup. The wow moment: viewer sees obligations they probably didn't know existed, surfaced specifically for this business.
1:00-2:30 - Phase 3 autonomous execution (LLC filing). Agent says "let's start with the LLC since everything else depends on it," opens Illinois SOS, fills business name/address/entity type/state autonomously while bilingual sidebar narrates in Chinese. Reaches management structure question, pauses, highlights field, asks user in Chinese with explanation. User answers by voice. Agent continues. Reaches submission, summarizes everything in Chinese, gets confirmation.
2:30-3:00 - Cross-language close-the-loop. Filing completes. Agent surfaces "next: EIN application - this is what unlocks your business banking. Should we do this now?" Brief gesture at starting the EIN flow to demonstrate the chain. Confirmation page translated to Chinese, next-step preview in user's language.
Three wow moments to script around.
The checklist appearing in phase 2. Twelve specific obligations the user didn't know about, with citations, generated from a 30-second conversation. This is the asymmetry-being-closed moment - it shows the agent doing consultation work that translation tools and chatbots can't.
Bilingual clarification on a judgment field in phase 3. Form pauses, field highlights, Chinese question appears with explanation of the trade-off, user responds by voice, field fills. Shows the agent is doing advisory work, not just translation.
Cross-language close-the-loop with chained next step. Filing completes, agent translates the confirmation, gestures at the next item in the checklist. Shows end-to-end completion AND that this is a workflow, not a transaction.
The critical timing detail: voice ending → form action needs to be sub-second for wow #2 to land. We achieve this by front-loadinintent extraction into a single Claude call (instant structured JSON of all field values) and triggering the overlay annotation render the moment that response returns - even before browser-use finishes the actual clicking sequence.
Architecture. Three concurrent loops orchestrated from a Python backend:
Voice loop. Whisper (transcription, streaming) → Claude API (intent extraction to structured field values via structured outputs). Sub-second target.
Operation loop. browser-use (the open-source library, 81k stars) as the autonomous browser agent, with Claude Sonnet 4.6 as the underlying LLM. Patterned closely on their apply_to_job.py example, which is structurally identical to what we need: structured user data + form URL + task description → autonomous form completion. Browser-use handles dynamic form discovery via extract_structured_data, popup/modal handling, file uploads, and the screenshot-decide-act-repeat loop we don't have to build. Built on Playwright underneath, so we have access to the underg page object for overlay injection.
Annotation overlay loop. React + Tailwind + Framer Motion, built as a self-contained JS bundle, injected into every page via Playwright's page.add_init_script() (accessed through browser-use's exposed Browser/page object). Rendered as an absolutely-positioned layer with pointer-events: none. Field positions come from DOM bounding boxes. Components: <HighlightLayer> (rings around active fields), <AnnotationBubble> (Chinese context cards positioned via Floating UI), <VoicePanel> (persistent sidebar for conversational state), <ChecklistView> (the phase 2 obligations list, persistent across phases), <ProgressIndicator> (workflow step counter). Animations via Framer Motion - this polish difference is what makes the overlay feel like a product instead of a debugger.
The clarification mechanism connects all three via a custom browser-use tool: ask_user_for_clarification(field_name, options, context). When the agent hits a judgment field, it calls this tool, which (a) updates the overlay to show the highlight and bilingual question, (b) opens the voice loop for user response, (c) returns the answer back to browser-use, which then continues filling. Tonight's most critical verification: confirm browser-use's tool hooks support async pauses for user input. If they don't, fall back to Plan B: front-load all clarifications in a conversational pre-flight before browser-use starts running, then hand it the complete structured data. Loses the "agent stops mid-flow to ask" demo moment but keeps everything else.
Phase 1 + Phase 2: the consultation engine.
Phase 1 is a conversational agent with a Claude system prompt designed to elicit the structured business profile. ~5 targeted questions covering business type, location specificity, structure, hiring plans, products/services, alcohol/food/regulated, retail vs wholesale, online presence. Outputs a structured profile JSON.
Phase 2 takes that profile and queries a curated requirements database (the new technical asset). Each requirement has: jurisdiction, eligibility logic, citation to underlying regulation, Chinese explanation, urgency/sequencing notes, link to the relevant filing portal, estimated time/cost. For the demo, we hand-curate ~25 requirements covering federal/Illinois/Cook County/Chicago for a single-member food-service LLC profile. The database doesn't need to be comprehensive - it needs to be correct for the demo case, with verifiable citations. This curation is the most important deliverable that isn't code, and it's what makes the pitch defensible against "you just asked Claude for a list."
Stack.
Orchestrator: Python (browser-use is Python-only).
Browser agent: browser-use library (pip install browser-use).
LLM: Anthropic Claude Sonnet 4.6 via ChatAnthropic (browser-use supports this directly - one-line swap from the OpenAI default in the example).
STT: OpenAI Whisper API (Python SDK), streaming mode for low latency.
TTS: ElevenLabs Multilingual v2 model (best Chinese quality). Pre-cache standard responses (intro, close, common clarification questions) so they play instantly during demo.
Overlay UI: React + Tailwind + Framer Motion + Floating UI (positioning library), built with Vite into a single JS bundle.
Requirements database: Hand-curated JSON, ~25 entries for the demo profile. Each entry: jurisdiction, eligibility predicate, citation URL, Chinese explanation, sequencing priority, filing portal URL.
Communication Python ↔ overlay: page.evaluate('window.updateOverlayState(...)') to push state from Python into the browser; page.expose_function('onUserVoice', handler) for voice input flowing back.
Scope discipline. One business profile (Chicago single-member restaurant LLC), one language pair (English ↔ Mandarin Chinese), one curated requirements database, one fully-demonstrated execution flow (LLC filing) with one gestured next step (EIN). Within that scope every judgment field the agent surfaces is hand-tuned, every checklist item is verified against real regulations with real citations, and the demo is rehearsed end-to. Don't try to generalize during build - but the pitch is honestly "the consultation works for any business profile in our database; the execution architecture works for any English-language form. We're showing this profile because it's the canonical example."
Pre-build de-risking (tonight, in order):
Pull browser-use, run apply_to_job example successfully on local machine. pip install browser-use && playwright install chromium. If it works clean in 30 minutes, the operation loop is essentially proven.
Swap the LLM in the example from ChatOpenAI(model='o3') to ChatAnthropic(model='claude-sonnet-4-6'). Confirm it still runs.
Verify access to underlying Playwright page object from browser-use's Browser. Their docs cover this; needed for overlay injection.
Test custom tool hooks for async pause. Define a trivial ask_user(question) tool, see if browser-use will pause its loop until the tool returns. Make-or-break for the clarification moments. If it doesn't work, switch to Plan B (front-loaded clarifications) and adjust demo design accordingly.
Walk through the actual Illinois SOS LLC filing flow on the real site. Document every screen, every field, note any captchas or auth gates that would break automation. Try one screen with browser-use to see if the .gov site is hostile.
Curate the requirements database for Chicago single-member restaurant LLC. ~25 obligations, federal + Illinois + Cook County + Chicago. Each one with real citation, verified URL, Chinese explanation. This is the deliverable that makes the pitch defensible and is most likely to be deprioritized under time pressure - assign it explicitly to one person.
Sketch the three-phase UI on paper. Phase 1 voice chat interface, phase 2 checklist reveal with citations, phase 3 form-filling overlay with persistent checklist sidebar. Make design decisions before building.
Confirm who's recording the Chinese voice - native speaker strongly preferred. If no team member speaks Chinese, find one tonight.
Time budget for the build (8 hours, 3 people).
Hour 1: Setup, browser-use running, swap to Claude, basic LLC site load, hello-world overlay injection. All three people sync.
Hours 2-4: Three parallel workstreams.
Person A: Browser-use integration - structured data flow, custom clarification tool, demo flow scripting for phase 3.
Person B: Overlay React app - all components built standalone first (HighlightLayer, AnnotationBubble, VoicePanel, ChecklistView, ProgressIndicator). Phase 1 chat UI also lives here.
Person C: Voice loop AND requirements database integration - Whisper streaming, Claude intent extraction with structured outputs, ElevenLabs TTS, voice clip pre-caching, plus loading the curated requirements JSON and wiring phase 2 generation logic.
Hours 5-6: Integration. Wire overlay state updates to browser-use action events. Wire voice input back to clarification tool. Wire phase 1→2→3 transitions. End-to-end first run. This hour usually goes sideways - factor in buffer.
Hours 6-7: Polish. Latency optimization (specifically the voice-end-to-overlay-re beat). Animation timing for the checklist reveal in phase 2. Recording Chinese voice clips with the native speaker. Scripting the exact demo flow word for word.
Hour 7-8: Recording the demo video, slides, dry-run twice.
Honest risks.
Requirements database quality is the make-or-break for the pitch. If the checklist has hallucinated items or wrong citations, the "we surface things you didn't know about" claim collapses. Hand-curate, verify each entry, don't generate.
Async tool hooks in browser-use - if they don't support pausing for user input mid-loop, the in-flow clarification moment dies and we fall back to front-loaded clarifications. Verify tonight.
CSP on the Illinois SOS site might block overlay injection. Workaround: launch browser with --disable-web-security, or use a Chrome extension instead of init script. Test with DevTools console tonight.
Voice → action latency has to be tight or wow #2 dissolves. Optimize for this specifically - intent extraction batched, overlay render on response, browseuse fills fields in parallel.
Real .gov sites break in unpredictable ways. Pre-record the successful run as the demo backbone; have a live walkthrough only as supplementary evidence if asked.
Chinese voice authenticity matters. Synthesized or non-native voices undercut the demo because judges who speak Chinese will notice. Use a real native speaker for the recording.
Pitch (30 seconds). "When a US-born founder starts a business, they get a consultation - a lawyer or family friend who tells them what to file, in what order, why. That conversation surfaces obligations they didn't know to ask about, and it costs $400. Immigrant founders systematically miss this consultation - they ask family who know partial answers, miss obligations, or operate informally for years. We built the consultation in your language. Talk to it about your business; it generates your specific obligation list with citations - things ChatGPT wouldn't surface because you didn't know to ask. Then it walks through filing each one, navigating the English forms while explaining each decision. Watch a Chinese-speaking restaurant owner go from 'I want to open a restaurant' to filed LLC in three minutes."
Anticipated judge objection and response. "Couldn't they just use ChatGPT in Chinese?" - "Three problems. First, ChatGPT answers questions you ask; the consultation surfaces obligations you didn't know to ask about. Second, ChatGPT advises but can't act - it can't open the Illinois SOS site and fill the form for you. Third, chat is structurally the wrong interaction model for operating a foreign-language website - it forces you to alternate between the chatbot tab and the form tab, translating between 'what was said' and 'where to do it.' Our overlay collapses those surfaces. Voice in, action out, with clarifications appearing spatially next to the fields they're about. That's a different category of tool, not a translation chatbot with better UI."

## Premise challenge

You're framing this as "we need to walk through more filings to be a business admin agent." Let me invert that.

**Look at what you already have:** Phase 2 generates a 12-item checklist (LLC, EIN, BOI, sales tax permit, Cook County DBA, Chicago business license, restaurant license, food sanitation cert, withholding registration, workers' comp, federal employment tax, IL-941). That checklist *is* the "business admin agent" framing. The asymmetry-being-closed wow already lives there.

Phase 3 is the proof-of-operation moment — and proof-of-operation is about depth, not breadth. One filing executed beautifully > two filings executed adequately. Judges have 3 minutes; doubling the form-fill content halves the polish on each.

**The real question isn't "walk through more filings." It's "how do we make the architecture's generality legible without spending demo seconds on it?"** Three of the four answers below don't require a second live walkthrough.

## What you already have working for you

- `corpus/requirements.py::DEMO_PROFILE` already drives the 12-item Phase 2 reveal — you've **already** built the multi-filing consultation engine.
- `corpus/il_llc_corpus.py` already abstracts fields via `FieldSpec` + `FieldKind`. Adding a second filing is a corpus instance, not a refactor.
- `agent/demo_runner.py:369-403` (`_stage_chained_next_step`) already opens the live IRS EIN page after LLC submit — the "chain" gesture is in.
- `agent/real_site_runner.py` is generic page-walking infrastructure, not LLC-specific.

You have ~80% of the "business admin agent" surface already. The question is which 20% is worth adding.

That's the right concern, and it's much more actionable than "expand the demo." You don't need to lengthen the recording — you need an **evidence stash for Q&A** that you can pull out in 30-60 seconds if asked.

## The judge's question has two halves

When someone asks "is this just LLC?", they're really asking two things:

1. **Does the consultation engine generalize?** (Phase 1+2 — the obligation map)
2. **Does the execution engine generalize?** (Phase 3 — the form-filling)

These have very different costs to prove. Half #1 is cheap and you're 80% there already. Half #2 is expensive because every new filing means new selectors and a new anti-bot fight.

## What you can leverage right now

- `agent/state.py:DemoState.business_profile` already parameterizes the flow per-profile.
- `corpus/requirements.py::requirements_for_profile(profile)` is already the engine that maps a `BusinessProfile` → checklist. **This already supports multi-profile** — you just only curated one (`DEMO_PROFILE` = single-member Chicago restaurant).
- The Phase 2 checklist already shows 12 obligations from a 30-second conversation. That's a *consultation-engine generality* proof you've already built and aren't fully selling.
- `_stage_chained_next_step` already opens the IRS EIN page after LLC submit. That's a *visual* hook for execution-engine generality.

So the cheapest, highest-leverage move is on Half #1, not Half #2.

## Four possible Q&A safety nets, ranked

Your instinct is good — and the "stop before payment" cut is the right one because payment is destructive anyway, so stopping there is natural rather than abandoned. Three things I want to push on before you commit:

## What I like about your plan

- The LLC backbone stays — wow #1 (checklist), wow #2 (clarification) untouched.
- "Same architecture, different filing" is exactly the verbal hook judges respond to.
- A SECOND filing visible on screen is qualitatively different from any verbal/architectural defense. Judges believe what they see.

## What I want to push on

**1. "Stop midway" is the weakest part of the framing.** Stopping midway always reads as "ran out of time" unless the cut is intentional. You need a natural beat point — fill 3 fields, hit one clarification, fill one more, then a Chinese voice-over says "rest of the form is the same pattern — let's keep going on your own time" + checklist ticks `1/12 → 2/12`. The visible counter is the difference between "demo ended" and "agent finished a unit."

**2. Deterministic vs browser-use for filing #2.** You said deterministic. That's faster to build and zero variance, but the pitch is slightly weaker because you're proving "our scripts generalize" rather than "the agent generalizes." Hybrid wins: browser-use for the first 1-2 fields (proves agent), deterministic for the rest (predictable). Same architectural story, much lower risk.

**3. The choice of filing #2 is binding and matters more than how you implement it.** Here's the real tradeoff matrix — IRS EIN is the obvious "next" in the chain narratively, but it's the worst engineering choice. Government sites have very different hostility profiles:

| Filing | Narrative fit | Site hostility | Selector cost | Honest hours |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IRS EIN** | Strongest (the chain story) | Very high (anti-bot, session timeouts) | High | 10-14h, high variance |
| **BOI report (FinCEN)** | Strong (topical 2024 obligation, *another* asymmetry-closed moment) | Medium | Medium | 7-10h |
| **IL sales tax (MyTax IL)** | Medium (restaurant sells food → sales tax) | Low (state portal, similar to IL SOS) | Low-medium | 5-7h |
| **Chicago business license** | Strong (local restaurant angle) | Medium (city portal quirks) | Medium | 7-9h |

**My read:** The narrative pull toward EIN is a trap. If your second filing breaks during recording or Q&A, the architecture-generalizes story dies harder than if you'd never tried. **IL sales tax is the safest second filing** — same state, similar portal patterns, minimal new failure surface — and the restaurant-sells-food angle gives it narrative legs even though it's not the obvious "next" item.

**4. Honest time accounting.** This isn't 2 hours. Realistically 5-10 hours depending on the filing, *plus* recording rehearsal (every demo runner change adds rehearsal time). That trades against LLC polish, voice latency tuning, and slack for last-mile bugs. If you're already past midnight on tonight's gates, this might be the wrong call regardless of how good the idea is.

## The decision

## Sketch of what the build looks like

```
[Existing] LLC walk → in-flow pause (wow #2) → continue → STOP at payment page
              ↓
         transition beat (~5s):
         "first filing complete. next: sales tax permit. same agent."
         checklist counter ticks 1/12
              ↓
[New]    Open MyTax Illinois → deterministic fill of 4-6 fields
         (autofill cascades from data already extracted in Phase 1)
         visible: business name, address, EIN, business type, NAICS
              ↓
         cut beat (~5s):
         checklist ticks 2/12, voiceover: "10 more in your checklist —
         same pattern. on your own time, in your own language."
              ↓
         closing screen
```

Total demo length: ~3:30-3:45 instead of ~3:00. Acceptable but tight.

## Critical gate before any build (don't skip)

**Walk MyTax Illinois manually first.** Specifically check for:

1. Does `https://mytax.illinois.gov/_/` (or the business registration entry point — REG-1) require account creation before you can see the form fields? If yes, the whole plan changes — you'd need to either stub an account or pivot to the public REG-1 paper-form-online flow.
2. CSP — does `--disable-web-security` allow overlay injection? (Same gate you ran on IL SOS.)
3. Anti-bot — does MyTax serve the form to headless+chromium, or only to real Chrome? (You already handle this for IL SOS live mode — same pattern should work.)
4. Are there CAPTCHAs on form pages? (IL SOS doesn't seem to have them; MyTax I'm less sure of.)

If gate 1 fails, fall back to the **IL DOR REG-1 online new business registration** as the second filing instead — it's pre-login and serves the same narrative.

This walk-through is ~30 minutes of work and binds everything else. Do it before writing any code.

## The depth/clarification decision

The biggest single design call now is whether filing #2 has its own clarification moment or not. Strong reasons to NOT add one:

- Wow #2 (the bilingual clarification on management structure) is unique because it's unique. A second clarification 60s later dilutes the move — judges feel "ah, another one of these" instead of "oh, that was THE moment."
- A second clarification doubles the engineering: voice routing, regex enums for sales-tax fields, more cached audio. ~3-4 extra hours.
- Keeping filing #2 as a fast silent fill cascade reads as **"same architecture just keeps going"** — which is precisely the architectural-generality argument you want.

## Concrete architecture

```
corpus/
  il_sales_tax_corpus.py     # NEW — FieldSpec list, all kind=AUTOFILL, ordered by demo priority
  __init__.py                # register second filing alongside IL_LLC
agent/
  demo_runner.py             # add _stage_sales_tax_walk after _stage_continue_and_submit
  state.py                   # stub federal_ein on LLCSchema (sales tax registration needs it)
  i18n.py                    # transition strings + sales-tax narration in zh/en
  pacing.py                  # add sales_tax_field_stagger_s + cut-point config
overlay/
  inject.js                  # checklist counter increment helper (small addition)
```

Cut-point control via env var:
```python
# In _stage_sales_tax_walk
max_fields = int(os.environ.get("SALES_TAX_MAX_FIELDS", len(IL_SALES_TAX_FIELDS)))
for field in IL_SALES_TAX_FIELDS[:max_fields]:
    await page.fill(field.selector_live, value)
    await asyncio.sleep(pacing.sales_tax_field_stagger_s)
```

During recording: `SALES_TAX_MAX_FIELDS=6 python main.py` to trim live without code changes.

## Field list (full walk — cut wherever feels right)

Roughly this order so cut points feel natural:

1. Federal EIN (stubbed `12-3456789` for the demo — you don't have a real one yet at this point in the chain)
2. Business legal name → from `entity_name`
3. Business address → from `principal_address`
4. City / state / ZIP → from schema
5. Business email / phone → from schema
6. Business structure → "LLC" (from `llc_type`)
7. **NAICS code** → "722511 — Full-Service Restaurants" (good visual moment: shows a `LOOKUP` FieldKind, not just autofill)
8. Business start date → today
9. Expected monthly sales → default "$10,000"
10. Filing frequency → default "Monthly"
11. Owner info → from `organizer_name`

Natural cut points after fields 4, 6, or 8. Field 7 (NAICS) is the most visually interesting because it's a lookup — gives you a small "the agent figured this out from 'restaurant'" beat without being a clarification.

## Build sequence (realistic ~4.5 hours)

The first 30 minutes is a pure GO/NO-GO recon. Don't write code before this gate clears:

- **Gate 0 — manual MyTax recon (30 min):** Visit `mytax.illinois.gov`, find the new business registration entry point, walk every screen with DevTools open. Confirm: (a) is the form publicly accessible without login, (b) does CSP allow injected scripts, (c) does it serve to headed Chrome with the same UA you use for IL SOS. If gate fails on (a), pivot to **IL DOR REG-1 paper-form-online** as the actual filing — same narrative.
- **Step 1 — corpus (45 min):** Write `il_sales_tax_corpus.py` with the field list + selectors captured during recon.
- **Step 2 — stage runner (1h):** `_stage_sales_tax_walk` modeled on `_stage_cold_open` — pure `page.fill()` cascade, no browser-use, no clarification. Add `SALES_TAX_MAX_FIELDS` env knob.
- **Step 3 — transition + checklist counter (1h):** Between LLC submit and sales tax: open MyTax in new tab, voiceover beat, increment checklist counter 1/12 → 2/12. New i18n strings.
- **Step 4 — end-to-end + bug fix (30 min):** First run, fix breakage.
- **Step 5 — recording rehearsal x2 (1h):** Tune the cut point. Probably land on 6-8 fields.

## What I'd recommend doing right now

The recon (Gate 0) is the fork in the road. Until you've walked MyTax Illinois manually, every implementation detail is speculation — including whether MyTax is the right second filing at all.

Two paths from here:

1. **You walk MyTax manually**, report back with what you see (login wall? form structure? captchas?), and I'll write the corpus + stage runner against the real selectors you captured.
2. **I run `scripts/recon_il_sos.py`-style automated recon** against MyTax Illinois (you already have a recon script pattern in the repo) to characterize the site automatically — then we both look at what comes back and decide.

Path 1 is faster and more reliable because human eyes catch login/captcha/multi-step flows that automated recon can miss. Path 2 is hands-off for you. Which one?

Yes — several. Ranked by fit for your demo:

## Strong candidates (no login)

| Form | Site | Login? | Anti-bot | Narrative fit | Engineering effort |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FinCEN BOI Report** | `boiefiling.fincen.gov` | None — public form | Mild (federal site, less hostile than IRS) | **Excellent** — topical, exactly the "things immigrant founders miss" story | Medium — new domain, fresh selector capture |
| **IL SOS Assumed Name** (LLC DBA) | `apps.ilsos.gov/llcassumedname/` (same domain as LLC) | None | Same as IL SOS LLC — already cracked | OK — DBAs are real but less prestigious | **Lowest** — same anti-bot patterns work, similar form structure |
| **IL SOS Articles of Amendment** | `apps.ilsos.gov` (same domain) | None | Same as LLC | Weak — "amend the LLC you just filed" is meta | Lowest |
| **IL SOS Annual Report** | `apps.ilsos.gov` (same domain) | None | Same as LLC | OK — every LLC has to do this yearly, recurring obligation angle | Low |

## Weaker candidates

| Form | Why not |
|---|---|
| **IRS EIN** (`sa.www4.irs.gov`) | Public but aggressive anti-bot, 15-min session timeouts, captcha-prone |
| **MyTax Illinois sales tax** | Login wall on most flows |
| **USPTO TEAS** (federal trademark) | Now requires USPTO.gov account (changed late 2023) |
| **Chicago BACP business license** | Account required |
| **Cook County DBA** | In-person / mail typically |

## My take

**Two real options, depending on what you optimize for:**

**🥇 BOI Report** — best *narrative*. BOI is federally mandated under the Corporate Transparency Act, became required January 2024, has been in court-driven legal limbo since, and is exactly the kind of obligation immigrant founders systematically miss. Walking through a BOI filing in the demo means *another* asymmetry-being-closed moment, not just architectural-generality proof. The form has SSN/ID-number fields that need stubbed test data. **Caveat: legal status of the BOI requirement has been bouncing around — verify it's currently active before leaning hard on the topicality angle in the pitch.**

**🥈 IL SOS Assumed Name (LLC DBA)** — best *engineering*. Same domain as your existing LLC filing (`apps.ilsos.gov`), so the anti-bot mitigations you already have just work. Selector capture is the fastest of any option. Highest probability of working at recording time and during a live Q&A demo. Narrative is weaker — DBAs aren't the most exciting filing — but you can frame it as "even the boring stuff: same agent, same architecture."

If your remaining build hours are tight: pick IL SOS Assumed Name. The probability-of-working-at-demo-time is so much higher than BOI that it dominates the narrative downside.

If you have ~8+ free hours: BOI. The narrative wow is real and it doubles as a Phase 2 callback ("remember the BOI report on the checklist? watch.").

## Current Status of FinCEN BOI Beneficial Ownership Reporting Requirements (2026)

All entities created in the United States — including those previously known as "domestic reporting companies" — and their beneficial owners are now exempt from the requirement to report beneficial ownership information (BOI) to FinCEN under the Corporate Transparency Act (CTA). This represents a significant change that affects small businesses.

### Key Changes for Small Businesses

On March 21, 2025, the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) has issued a new regulation that exempts all U.S. small businesses and U.S. persons from the Beneficial Ownership Information (BOI) reporting requirements. This means that small businesses do not need to file federal BOI reports.

### Current Scope of Requirements

Following the March 2025 interim final rule, FinCEN revised the definition of "reporting company" to mean only those entities formed under the law of a foreign country that have registered to do business in any U.S. State or tribal jurisdiction by the filing of a document with a secretary of state or similar office. Reporting companies now also do not need to report the BOI of any U.S. persons, and U.S. persons are exempt from having to provide BOI with respect to any reporting company for which they are a beneficial owner.

### Deadlines for Foreign Entities

Only foreign entities that meet the new definition must report. Reporting companies registered to do business in the United States before March 26, 2025, must file BOI reports by April 25, 2025. Reporting companies registered to do business in the United States on or after March 26, 2025, have 30 calendar days to file an initial BOI report after receiving notice that their registration is effective.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Critical finding before I touch any code:

## BOI requirement was killed for US small businesses

**FinCEN issued an interim final rule on March 21, 2025** that exempts all U.S.-formed entities — including the Chicago single-member restaurant LLC in your demo — from BOI reporting. Only foreign entities registered to do business in the U.S. must now file.

This kills the narrative that made BOI attractive. The pitch "we surface obligations immigrant founders miss — like BOI" doesn't work when BOI doesn't apply to your demo business. A sharp judge who follows small-business compliance news (and some will) catches this immediately, and the asymmetry-closed framing collapses.

**Making the reasonable call: pivoting to IL SOS Assumed Name** — the previously-ranked engineering-safest option. Same domain as your LLC filing, anti-bot mitigations already work, lowest selector capture cost, ~4 hours realistic. The narrative downgrade ("DBAs are real but less prestigious than BOI") is a smaller hit than "the obligation we surfaced doesn't actually apply." Redirect me if you'd rather stick with BOI despite this — there's still an argument for it as a pure architectural-generality demo, just without the narrative wow.

- [FinCEN Removes Beneficial Ownership Reporting Requirements for U.S. Companies and U.S. Persons](https://www.fincen.gov/news/news-releases/fincen-removes-beneficial-ownership-reporting-requirements-us-companies-and-us)
- [Federal Register: BOI Reporting Requirement Revision and Deadline Extension (March 26, 2025)](https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2025/03/26/2025-05199/beneficial-ownership-information-reporting-requirement-revision-and-deadline-extension)
- [NFIB: BOI Victory — FinCEN Removes BOI Reporting Requirement for America's Small Businesses](https://www.nfib.com/news/legal-blog/beneficial-ownership-information-boi-victory-fincen-removes-boi-reporting-requirement-for-americas-small-businesses/)

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Illinois Secretary of State LLC assumed name filing through apps.ilsos.gov:

## Online Filing Applications

The Illinois Secretary of State provides an online application for adopting a Limited Liability Company's Assumed Name at apps.ilsos.gov/llcassumedadoptname/, and there is also an LLC Assumed Name Renewal application available at apps.ilsos.gov/llassrenewal/.

## Eligibility and Filing

Domestic and foreign limited liability companies that are in good standing may adopt an assumed name electronically.

## Effective Period and Fees

An assumed name may be adopted in five-year increments, and the right to use an assumed name shall be effective from the date of filing by the Secretary of State until the first day of the anniversary month of the Limited Liability Company that falls within the next calendar year evenly divisible by 5.

The filing fee to adopt an assumed name is $150 for each year or part thereof ending in 0 or 5; $120 for each year or part thereof ending in 1 or 6; $90 for each year or part thereof ending in 2 or 7; $60 for each year or part thereof ending in other digits.

## Payment

Payment must be made using Visa, MasterCard, Discover or American Express credit card.

101	async def capture_page(page: Page, step: int, label: str) -> dict:
102	    RECON_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
103	    title = await page.title()
104	    url = page.url
105	
106	    await asyncio.sleep(1.0)  # let the page settle
107	
108	    info = await page.evaluate(
109	        r"""() => {
110	          const labelFor = (el) => {
111	            let lbl = '';
112	            if (el.id) {
113	              const l = document.querySelector(`label[for="${el.id}"]`);
114	              if (l) lbl = l.innerText.trim();
115	            }
116	            if (!lbl) {
117	              const pl = el.closest('label');
118	              if (pl) lbl = pl.innerText.trim();
119	            }
120	            return lbl.replace(/\s+/g, ' ').slice(0, 200);
121	          };
122	          const inputs = Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('input, select, textarea')).map(el => ({
123	            tag: el.tagName.toLowerCase(),
124	            type: el.type || null,
125	            name: el.name || null,
126	            id: el.id || null,
127	            value: (el.value || '').slice(0, 100),
128	            placeholder: el.placeholder || null,
129	            label: labelFor(el),
130	            required: !!el.required,
131	            visible: !!(el.offsetWidth || el.offsetHeight || el.getClientRects().length),
132	          }));
133	          const buttons = Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('button, input[type=submit], input[type=button], a[role=button]'))
134	            .map(el => ({
135	              tag: el.tagName.toLowerCase(),
136	              type: el.type || null,
137	              text: (el.innerText || el.value || '').trim().slice(0, 100),
138	              id: el.id || null,
139	              name: el.name || null,
140	            }))
141	            .filter(b => b.text);
142	          const bodyText = document.body.innerText.slice(0, 2000);
143	          return { inputs, buttons, bodyText };
144	        }"""
145	    )
146	
147	    screenshot_path = RECON_DIR / f"step{step:02d}-{label}.png"
148	    await page.screenshot(path=str(screenshot_path))
149	
150	    return {
151	        "step": step,
152	        "label": label,
153	        "url": url,
154	        "title": title,
155	        "screenshot": str(screenshot_path),
156	        "inputs": info["inputs"],
157	        "buttons": info["buttons"],
158	        "body_excerpt": info["bodyText"][:600],
159	    }

162	def fuzzy_seed_value(field: dict) -> str | None:
163	    """Match an input's id/name/label against SEED_VALUES."""
164	    haystacks = [
165	        (field.get("id") or "").lower(),
166	        (field.get("name") or "").lower(),
167	        (field.get("label") or "").lower(),
168	        (field.get("placeholder") or "").lower(),
169	    ]
170	    for key, value in SEED_VALUES.items():
171	        for h in haystacks:
172	            if not h:
173	                continue
174	            if key in h:
175	                return value
176	    return None
177	
178	
179	async def fill_defaults(page: Page, info: dict) -> dict:
180	    """Fill all visible inputs with reasonable defaults. Returns audit log."""
181	    actions = []
182	    # Group radios by name so we only check ONE per group.
183	    radio_groups: dict[str, list[dict]] = {}
184	    for f in info["inputs"]:
185	        if not f["visible"]:
186	            continue
187	        if f["type"] == "radio":
188	            radio_groups.setdefault(f["name"] or "_unnamed", []).append(f)
189	
190	    # Default radio selection — prefer "Yes" / "Standard" / first option.
191	    for name, group in radio_groups.items():
192	        # Don't overwrite if something already checked
193	        already = await page.evaluate(
194	            f"""() => Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('input[type=radio][name="{name}"]'))
195	                       .some(r => r.checked)"""
196	        )
197	        if already:
198	            continue
199	        # Look for Yes / Standard / Member-managed first, else first option
200	        chosen = None
201	        for r in group:
202	            label = (r.get("label") or "").lower()
203	            if any(kw in label for kw in [" yes", "yes ", "agree", "accept", "standard", "member"]):
204	                chosen = r
205	                break
206	        chosen = chosen or group[0]
207	        sel = f"#{chosen['id']}" if chosen.get("id") else f'input[name="{chosen["name"]}"][value="{chosen.get("value", "")}"]'
208	        try:
209	            await page.check(sel)
210	            actions.append({"action": "check_radio", "selector": sel, "label": chosen.get("label")})
211	        except Exception as e:
212	            actions.append({"action": "check_radio_failed", "selector": sel, "error": str(e)})
213	
214	    # Detect validation errors — if any are visible, we must REFILL even
215	    # fields that have stale values (the form rejected them last time).
216	    has_errors = await page.evaluate(
217	        """() => Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('.error, .errorMessage, [class*=error]'))
218	                  .some(e => /required|invalid|must|please/i.test(e.innerText || ''))"""
219	    )
220	
221	    # Fill text inputs.
222	    for f in info["inputs"]:
223	        if not f["visible"]:
224	            continue
225	        ftype = f["type"]
226	        if ftype not in (None, "text", "email", "tel", "number"):
227	            continue
228	        if f["tag"] != "input":
229	            # textarea also OK
230	            if f["tag"] != "textarea":
231	                continue
232	        if f.get("value") and not has_errors:
233	            continue
234	        sel = f"#{f['id']}" if f.get("id") else (
235	            f'[name="{f["name"]}"]' if f.get("name") else None
236	        )
237	        if not sel:
238	            continue
239	        value = fuzzy_seed_value(f)
240	        if not value:
241	            actions.append({"action": "skipped_no_match", "label": f.get("label"), "id": f.get("id"), "name": f.get("name")})
242	            continue
243	        try:
244	            # Clear first if there's a stale value from a previous step
245	            if f.get("value"):
246	                await page.fill(sel, "")
247	            await page.fill(sel, value)
248	            actions.append({"action": "fill", "selector": sel, "value": value, "label": f.get("label")})
249	        except Exception as e:
250	            actions.append({"action": "fill_failed", "selector": sel, "error": str(e)})
251	
252	    # Fill selects with sensible defaults — context-aware by id/name/label.
253	    PLACEHOLDER_RX = re.compile(r"^(\s*-+\s*|select\s+one|please\s+select|choose|--.*--)\s*$", re.I)
254	    for f in info["inputs"]:
255	        if f["tag"] != "select" or not f["visible"]:
256	            continue
257	        sel = f"#{f['id']}" if f.get("id") else f'[name="{f["name"]}"]'
258	        if not sel:
259	            continue
260	        haystack = " ".join([(f.get("id") or ""), (f.get("name") or ""), (f.get("label") or "")]).lower()
261	        try:
262	            options = await page.evaluate(
263	                f"""() => Array.from(document.querySelector('{sel}').options)
264	                                .map(o => ({{ value: o.value, text: o.text }}))"""
265	            )
266	            # Drop placeholder options
267	            real = [
268	                o for o in options
269	                if o["value"].strip()
270	                and not PLACEHOLDER_RX.match(o["text"] or "")
271	                and not PLACEHOLDER_RX.match(o["value"] or "")
272	            ]
273	            if not real:
274	                continue
275	            # State dropdown? Prefer Illinois.
276	            if "state" in haystack:
277	                pick = next(
278	                    (o for o in real if o["value"].upper() in ("IL", "ILLINOIS") or "illinois" in (o["text"] or "").lower()),
279	                    real[0],
280	                )
281	            elif "duration" in haystack or "perpet" in haystack:
282	                pick = next((o for o in real if "perpet" in (o["value"] or "").lower() or "perpet" in (o["text"] or "").lower()), real[0])
283	            elif "country" in haystack:
284	                pick = next(
285	                    (o for o in real if o["value"].upper() in ("US", "USA", "UNITED STATES") or "united states" in (o["text"] or "").lower()),
286	                    real[0],
287	                )
288	            else:
289	                pick = real[0]
290	            await page.select_option(sel, pick["value"])
291	            actions.append({"action": "select", "selector": sel, "value": pick["value"], "text": pick["text"]})
292	        except Exception as e:
293	            actions.append({"action": "select_failed", "selector": sel, "error": str(e)})
294	
295	    return {"actions": actions}
296	
297	
298	async def find_continue_button(page: Page) -> Any | None:
299	    """Find a Continue/Next button that ISN'T a final submit."""
300	    candidates = await page.query_selector_all(
301	        'button, input[type="submit"], input[type="button"], a[role="button"]'
302	    )

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-10 19:38:59Z)**_

WebFetch hit 403 — expected, since `apps.ilsos.gov` rejects non-Chrome UAs. Same anti-bot as the LLC site, which is actually good news: the mitigations you already have work.

The right move is to fork your existing `scripts/recon_il_sos.py` to target the assumed-name URL and run it locally — that's the only way to get past the anti-bot and capture real selectors. One concern I want to flag now so we both know:

**Likely complication:** IL SOS Assumed Name forms typically ask for the **existing LLC's File Number** on page 1 (because you're adopting an assumed name *for* an already-filed LLC). Shu Xiang LLC doesn't have a real file number — the existing demo either uses the mock or stops before submit. We'll need a stubbed file number that's at least *format-valid* (typically 8 digits) and hope page 1 doesn't validate it against the actual SOS database. If page 1 does immediate validation, we either (a) pick a real public LLC's file number for the demo (awkward), or (b) skip the validation page in recording by jumping to a deep link if one exists, or (c) pre-fill it via Playwright and accept that submission would fail — fine for our purposes since we stop before submit anyway.

Recon script will tell us which case we're in. Writing it now and running with `--max-steps 3` (just capture the first few pages, don't try to actually file). Heads-up: this opens a visible Chrome window briefly.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-10 19:39:37Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```python
"""
Walk the IL SOS LLC Assumed Name (DBA) filing flow and capture structure.

Forked from recon_il_sos.py. Same anti-bot strategy (real Chrome + UA), same
capture+JSON dump pattern. Stops at any terminal/payment button OR validation
failure (which is likely on page 1 since we don't have a real LLC file number).

Output:
  out/recon_assumed_name/step{NN}-{label}.png   screenshots
  out/recon_assumed_name/flow.json              full structured dump

Usage:
    python scripts/recon_il_assumed_name.py [--max-steps 3]
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import asyncio
import json
import re
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any

from playwright.async_api import Page, async_playwright


UA = (
    "Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 14_5) "
    "AppleWebKit/605.1.15 (KHTML, like Gecko) Version/17.5 Safari/605.1.15"
)
START_URL = "https://apps.ilsos.gov/llcassumedadoptname/"
RECON_DIR = Path("out/recon_assumed_name")

# Seed values — same restaurant LLC story as the existing demo. File number
# is stubbed; the form will reject it on submit but we stop well before that.
SEED_VALUES = {
    # The likely-required page-1 field
    "file number": "12345678",
    "file no": "12345678",
    "filing number": "12345678",

    "llcname": "Shu Xiang LLC",
    "llc name": "Shu Xiang LLC",
    "company name": "Shu Xiang LLC",
    "entity name": "Shu Xiang LLC",

    # The assumed name itself — the restaurant's storefront brand
    "assumed name": "Shu Xiang Kitchen",
    "dba": "Shu Xiang Kitchen",
    "doing business as": "Shu Xiang Kitchen",

    "principal place": "123 W Randolph St",
    "address1": "123 W Randolph St",
    "street": "123 W Randolph St",
    "city": "Chicago",
    "zip": "60601",
    "state": "IL",
    "address": "123 W Randolph St",

    "organizer name": "Wei Zhang",
    "applicant name": "Wei Zhang",
    "email": "wei@shuxiangchicago.com",
    "phone": "3125551212",
}


PAYMENT_INPUT_PATTERNS = [
    r"card[_-]?number",
    r"creditcard",
    r"cc[_-]?num",
    r"\bcvv\b",
    r"\bcvc\b",
    r"expir",
    r"cardholder",
]
TERMINAL_BUTTON_TEXT = [
    "submit filing",
    "submit application",
    "file assumed name",
    "pay now",
    "make payment",
    "process payment",
]


async def capture_page(page: Page, step: int, label: str) -> dict:
    RECON_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    title = await page.title()
    url = page.url

    await asyncio.sleep(1.0)

    info = await page.evaluate(
        r"""() => {
          const labelFor = (el) => {
            let lbl = '';
            if (el.id) {
              const l = document.querySelector(`label[for="${el.id}"]`);
              if (l) lbl = l.innerText.trim();
            }
            if (!lbl) {
              const pl = el.closest('label');
              if (pl) lbl = pl.innerText.trim();
            }
            return lbl.replace(/\s+/g, ' ').slice(0, 200);
          };
          const inputs = Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('input, select, textarea')).map(el => ({
            tag: el.tagName.toLowerCase(),
            type: el.type || null,
            name: el.name || null,
            id: el.id || null,
            value: (el.value || '').slice(0, 100),
            placeholder: el.placeholder || null,
            label: labelFor(el),
            required: !!el.required,
            visible: !!(el.offsetWidth || el.offsetHeight || el.getClientRects().length),
          }));
          const buttons = Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('button, input[type=submit], input[type=button], a[role=button]'))
            .map(el => ({
              tag: el.tagName.toLowerCase(),
              type: el.type || null,
              text: (el.innerText || el.value || '').trim().slice(0, 100),
              id: el.id || null,
              name: el.name || null,
            }))
            .filter(b => b.text);
          const bodyText = document.body.innerText.slice(0, 2000);
          return { inputs, buttons, bodyText };
        }"""
    )

    screenshot_path = RECON_DIR / f"step{step:02d}-{label}.png"
    await page.screenshot(path=str(screenshot_path))

    return {
        "step": step,
        "label": label,
        "url": url,
        "title": title,
        "screenshot": str(screenshot_path),
        "inputs": info["inputs"],
        "buttons": info["buttons"],
        "body_excerpt": info["bodyText"][:600],
    }


def fuzzy_seed_value(field: dict) -> str | None:
    haystacks = [
        (field.get("id") or "").lower(),
        (field.get("name") or "").lower(),
        (field.get("label") or "").lower(),
        (field.get("placeholder") or "").lower(),
    ]
    for key, value in SEED_VALUES.items():
        for h in haystacks:
            if not h:
                continue
            if key in h:
                return value
    return None


async def fill_defaults(page: Page, info: dict) -> dict:
    actions = []
    radio_groups: dict[str, list[dict]] = {}
    for f in info["inputs"]:
        if not f["visible"]:
            continue
        if f["type"] == "radio":
            radio_groups.setdefault(f["name"] or "_unnamed", []).append(f)

    for name, group in radio_groups.items():
        already = await page.evaluate(
            f"""() => Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('input[type=radio][name="{name}"]'))
                       .some(r => r.checked)"""
        )
        if already:
            continue
        chosen = None
        for r in group:
            label = (r.get("label") or "").lower()
            if any(kw in label for kw in [" yes", "yes ", "agree", "accept", "standard"]):
                chosen = r
                break
        chosen = chosen or group[0]
        sel = f"#{chosen['id']}" if chosen.get("id") else f'input[name="{chosen["name"]}"][value="{chosen.get("value", "")}"]'
        try:
            await page.check(sel)
            actions.append({"action": "check_radio", "selector": sel, "label": chosen.get("label")})
        except Exception as e:
            actions.append({"action": "check_radio_failed", "selector": sel, "error": str(e)})

    has_errors = await page.evaluate(
        """() => Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('.error, .errorMessage, [class*=error]'))
                  .some(e => /required|invalid|must|please/i.test(e.innerText || ''))"""
    )

    for f in info["inputs"]:
        if not f["visible"]:
            continue
        ftype = f["type"]
        if ftype not in (None, "text", "email", "tel", "number"):
            continue
        if f["tag"] != "input":
            if f["tag"] != "textarea":
                continue
        if f.get("value") and not has_errors:
            continue
        sel = f"#{f['id']}" if f.get("id") else (
            f'[name="{f["name"]}"]' if f.get("name") else None
        )
        if not sel:
            continue
        value = fuzzy_seed_value(f)
        if not value:
            actions.append({"action": "skipped_no_match", "label": f.get("label"), "id": f.get("id"), "name": f.get("name")})
            continue
        try:
            if f.get("value"):
                await page.fill(sel, "")
            await page.fill(sel, value)
            actions.append({"action": "fill", "selector": sel, "value": value, "label": f.get("label")})
        except Exception as e:
            actions.append({"action": "fill_failed", "selector": sel, "error": str(e)})

    PLACEHOLDER_RX = re.compile(r"^(\s*-+\s*|select\s+one|please\s+select|choose|--.*--)\s*$", re.I)
    for f in info["inputs"]:
        if f["tag"] != "select" or not f["visible"]:
            continue
        sel = f"#{f['id']}" if f.get("id") else f'[name="{f["name"]}"]'
        if not sel:
            continue
        haystack = " ".join([(f.get("id") or ""), (f.get("name") or ""), (f.get("label") or "")]).lower()
        try:
            options = await page.evaluate(
                f"""() => Array.from(document.querySelector('{sel}').options)
                                .map(o => ({{ value: o.value, text: o.text }}))"""
            )
            real = [
                o for o in options
                if o["value"].strip()
                and not PLACEHOLDER_RX.match(o["text"] or "")
                and not PLACEHOLDER_RX.match(o["value"] or "")
            ]
            if not real:
                continue
            if "state" in haystack:
                pick = next(
                    (o for o in real if o["value"].upper() in ("IL", "ILLINOIS") or "illinois" in (o["text"] or "").lower()),
                    real[0],
                )
            elif "country" in haystack:
                pick = next(
                    (o for o in real if o["value"].upper() in ("US", "USA", "UNITED STATES") or "united states" in (o["text"] or "").lower()),
                    real[0],
                )
            else:
                pick = real[0]
            await page.select_option(sel, pick["value"])
            actions.append({"action": "select", "selector": sel, "value": pick["value"], "text": pick["text"]})
        except Exception as e:
            actions.append({"action": "select_failed", "selector": sel, "error": str(e)})

    return {"actions": actions}


async def find_continue_button(page: Page) -> Any | None:
    candidates = await page.query_selector_all(
        'button, input[type="submit"], input[type="button"], a[role="button"]'
    )
    for btn in candidates:
        try:
            text = ((await btn.inner_text()) or (await btn.get_attribute("value")) or "").strip().lower()
        except Exception:
            text = ""
        if not text:
            continue
        if any(kw in text for kw in TERMINAL_BUTTON_TEXT):
            return None
        if any(kw in text for kw in ["continue", "next", "proceed"]):
            return btn
    return None


async def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--max-steps", type=int, default=3)
    args = parser.parse_args()

    async with async_playwright() as pw:
        try:
            browser = await pw.chromium.launch(
                channel="chrome",
                headless=False,
                args=["--disable-blink-features=AutomationControlled"],
            )
        except Exception:
            browser = await pw.chromium.launch(
                headless=False,
                args=["--disable-blink-features=AutomationControlled"],
            )

        ctx = await browser.new_context(
            viewport={"width": 1440, "height": 900},
            user_agent=UA,
            locale="en-US",
        )
        page = await ctx.new_page()
        steps: list[dict] = []

        try:
            await page.goto(START_URL, wait_until="domcontentloaded", timeout=20000)
            for step in range(1, args.max_steps + 1):
                info = await capture_page(page, step, f"page{step:02d}")
                steps.append(info)

                payment_input_hit = None
                for f in info["inputs"]:
                    if not f["visible"]:
                        continue
                    haystack = " ".join([(f.get("id") or ""), (f.get("name") or ""), (f.get("label") or "")]).lower()
                    for pat in PAYMENT_INPUT_PATTERNS:
                        if re.search(pat, haystack):
                            payment_input_hit = (pat, f)
                            break
                    if payment_input_hit:
                        break
                terminal_btn = None
                for b in info["buttons"]:
                    btext = (b.get("text") or "").lower()
                    if any(kw in btext for kw in TERMINAL_BUTTON_TEXT):
                        terminal_btn = b
                        break
                if payment_input_hit or terminal_btn:
                    info["stop_reason"] = (
                        f"payment input: {payment_input_hit[0]}" if payment_input_hit
                        else f"terminal button: {terminal_btn['text']!r}"
                    )
                    print(f"\n[STOP] step {step}: {info['stop_reason']}")
                    break

                print(f"\n=== STEP {step}: {info['title']} ===")
                print(f"  URL: {info['url']}")
                visible_inputs = [f for f in info['inputs'] if f['visible']]
                print(f"  Inputs ({len(visible_inputs)}):")
                for f in visible_inputs:
                    sel = f"#{f['id']}" if f.get("id") else (f'[name="{f["name"]}"]' if f.get("name") else "?")
                    print(f"    {sel}  type={f['type']}  label={f['label']!r}")
                print(f"  Buttons:")
                for b in info["buttons"][:8]:
                    print(f"    {b['text']!r}")

                fill_log = await fill_defaults(page, info)
                info["fill_actions"] = fill_log["actions"]

                btn = await find_continue_button(page)
                if not btn:
                    info["stop_reason"] = "no continue button"
                    print(f"  [STOP] no Continue button — likely review/submit page")
                    break
                btn_text = ((await btn.inner_text()) or "").strip()
                print(f"  [click] {btn_text!r}")
                try:
                    async with page.expect_navigation(wait_until="domcontentloaded", timeout=15000):
                        await btn.click()
                except Exception as e:
                    print(f"  [warn] no nav after click: {e}")
                    await asyncio.sleep(1.5)
                    new_url = page.url
                    if new_url == info["url"]:
                        info["stop_reason"] = "stuck on same URL after fill (likely file number validation failed)"
                        print(f"  [STOP] stuck on same page — file number probably rejected")
                        errors = await page.evaluate(
                            "() => Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('.error, .errorMessage, [class*=error]'))"
                            "       .map(e => e.innerText.trim()).filter(t => t).slice(0, 5)"
                        )
                        info["validation_errors"] = errors
                        print(f"  validation errors: {errors}")
                        break

        finally:
            (RECON_DIR / "flow.json").write_text(json.dumps(steps, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False))
            print(f"\nSaved {len(steps)} steps to {RECON_DIR / 'flow.json'}")
            await asyncio.sleep(2)
            await browser.close()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())

```

This is solvable, but I want to make the right call without burning more of your time on speculation. Let me lay out the situation crisply:

## The actual constraint

For RECORDING the demo, you've already been using `TARGET_ENV=mock` against `demo/il_sos_mock.html` for the LLC walk. The recording isn't hitting the live IL SOS site — it's hitting your local mock. Live mode exists for credibility, not for recording.

That means the right answer for filing #2 follows the same pattern: **build `demo/il_sos_assumed_name_mock.html` mirroring the real IL SOS Assumed Name form fields, walk through that.** Same architectural argument as your LLC mock. Live mode for filing #2 is best-effort (would need a real file number) but recording is bulletproof.

## What I need: field structure for the real form

I can't get past page 1 of the live form without a real file number. But IL SOS publishes the paper-form equivalent (**LLC-120 — Application to Adopt an Assumed Name**) as a PDF, which enumerates the same fields. Let me fetch it now to build the mock against real field structure:

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-7 2026-05-10 19:44:54Z)**_

Two quick notes before I act:

**EIN format flag:** A real EIN is 9 digits (`XX-XXXXXXX`). You wrote `41-254903` which is 8 digits — probably missing one digit at the end. If we use this on a form with real-time IRS validation, it'll fail. I'll assume it's `41-254903X` for now and treat the last digit as TBD — please confirm or share the full number when convenient.

**On the pivot — no mocks, find a real fillable form:**

Given the user just provided an EIN, the form that makes the most sense to target is one where **EIN is a primary input** rather than something that requires a prior IL SOS file number lookup. Two real candidates:

1. **MyTax Illinois REG-1 (sales tax / business registration)** — I dismissed it earlier assuming login wall, but I never actually verified whether the new-business registration intake has a public path. Sales tax permit takes EIN as input and is exactly the kind of "next-in-checklist" obligation after LLC formation. If this is publicly accessible, it's the strongest choice — no anti-bot, narrative fit, EIN-driven.
2. **IRS EIN Online Application (`sa.www4.irs.gov/modiein/`)** — Public, no login. Doesn't *require* a prior EIN (it creates one), but accepts an existing EIN if you're applying for a related entity. Anti-bot is real but not insurmountable. Strongest narrative continuity from LLC.

Let me verify MyTax Illinois has a public path now, plus check IRS EIN public accessibility in parallel:

Based on the search results, here's what I found about registering a new business with MyTax Illinois Form REG-1 without a pre-existing account:

## Registering Without an Account

You can click "Register a New Business" without needing an account yet. You can register through MyTax Illinois by selecting "Register a New Business (Form REG-1)" in the Register panel on the MyTax Illinois homepage and complete Form REG-1, Illinois Business Registration Application.

## Registration Steps

The process involves:

Entering your EIN (or SSN for sole proprietors), legal business name, and DBA (if applicable). You'll also need to provide your start date for sales, payroll, or business operations in Illinois, choose which taxes you'll be responsible for, and add info for each location where you operate in Illinois.

## Processing Time

Processing time for a registration application submitted through MyTax Illinois is approximately one to two business days.

## After Registration

Please allow 24 hours from your registration confirmation email before activating a logon for MyTax Illinois. To activate a logon for business, go to mytax.illinois.gov, select the "Sign Up" link, and then select the taxpayer ID type.

The online registration process at **mytax.illinois.gov** is designed to be accessible to new businesses that don't yet have an account.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about IRS EIN online applications:

## IRS EIN Online Application Overview

You can use the IRS tool to get an EIN directly from the IRS in minutes for free by answering questions and submitting the application, and if it's approved, they'll issue your EIN immediately online.

## Access Point

The online application can be found at the IRS website. You can go to the IRS website at IRS.gov/EIN. The sa.www4.irs.gov domain you mentioned appears to be the secure gateway for the application tool.

## Login Requirements

Regarding login requirements: The principal officer, general partner, grantor, owner, trustor, etc., must have a valid taxpayer identification number (SSN, EIN, or ITIN) in order to use the online application. This means you don't need a separate username/password login, but you will need to provide identifying information.

## Important Application Details

- You must complete the application in one session. You can't save it for later. It expires after 15 minutes of inactivity, and you'll need to start over.

- You should beware of websites that charge for an EIN. You never have to pay a fee for an EIN.

- You can apply for only 1 EIN per responsible party per day.

For starting a business in 2026, the process remains the same as described in the official IRS documentation.
